item.title: Licensing Captain Post
item.content:encoded: Article has been locked due to the author's previous and unnoticed subversion of an image takedown.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
There is no indication of the image being released under a license compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0. Image has since been removed.

Please do not reupload the image without consulting Licensing Staff first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wish I had me some instent algators. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very weird but not ridiculous enough to be a -J. 'instent algator' made me upvote.

Since 'algators' understand language, it would be interesting for the Foundation to attempt to interview one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I won't lie. I laughed at the title of this document. But the description is very interesting to me, in a way, so I very much liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found it kinda hilarious that they thought of using it as a researcher before they thought of using one as a D-class.

Also, that's not a tag line. That's a jingle.

item.title: Curious Regarding the Survivability of SCP-3579-2
item.content:encoded:
I am somewhat confused as to the longevity of SCP-3579-2. You have stated the alligator would suffer from a myocardial infarct if left to dehydrate for more than 5 minutes. However, you also record SCP-3579-2 remaining outside of water for 10 minutes before they return. I was wondering, do the alligators have some method to scan for nearby water, and if unable to find a way back to said water, suffer from the infarction? Personally, it seems a bit off that they can survive longer when they are in the know of a way back to their spawn location.

On an unrelated and silly note, would SCP-3579-2 be able to materialise inside other liquids? Not necessarily substances such as gasoline or elements like mercury, but how about orange juice? It does contain water in itself, albeit a lower amount than just lake or tap water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the purposes of testing whether an alligator would attack something, is it strictly necessary to have them attack D-Class personnel? I can understand if they were being used to prevent entry to another room as part of a test, but to simply say "hey, I wonder if we can use these to kill someone" seems like a waste of resources.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Source: http://www.desicomments.com/wp-content/uploads/American-Alligator-db033.jpg

I found it while looking up alligators.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I lol'd, so I made an audio version of it. I hope you enjoy it, great work author!

https://youtu.be/iBZxqZN8pBI

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: As no source was linked within the time limit, the image has been removed and the author notified. They are free to add it back if a complient source can be found.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Name: instent algator

And… upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But it still makes no sense why to have alligators do research. I suppose you want to go for surreal, but this is too much of a leap to suppose they are sapient when all they did thus far was to attack and not attack. That could be a climax point, but there should be some buildup to it.

P.S. It's Lunar Area 32.

item.title: Staff Post - Tag Reversion (Open)
item.content:encoded:
Noting to have reverted the tags. Of which, the tags (alligator, phone, telephone) are invalid tags.

A staff member will eventually tag an untagged article on your behalf. Unless you are perfectly aware of all the tags you need for an SCP article, refrain from doing so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hooray! It means a lot to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: eyyy, this is excellent! firm +1 from me, exceptional work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello! I listened to your critique and changed some information to make the gators try to complete a 'special request' if asked. I also made it so that if nothing is requested, then they will remain in the water, but stay hostile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello! I listened to your critique and changed some of the writing, and removed lots of redundant info.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the test logs would be better if there is a stated purpose for the gators to perform and the gators attempt to fulfil them to the best of their limited ability. Currently, it feels limited to a thing that summons things that kill. Making it specific to humans does not solve the issue.

The cross-test with 1161 feels weak. And why would there be another SCP in the vicinity when the SCP is proven to be capable of aggression. Perhaps not always destructive intentionally, but collateral damage is the real threat here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where'd they get the clipboard idea? How is that useful?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept here is great; the execution, not so much. The description is far from elegant - that is, it's extremely hamfisted and doesn't convey the information in a clinical or analytical manner. It's rough, there's a URL in there that feels completely unnecessary, and overall it just feels like it could with a good deal more polish. The tests were by far the most enjoyable part of the article for me, and most everything there reeks of 2875, which is a good thing. If this doesn't push through I would recommend continuing to work with just in the capacity that you shore up the writing in general and especially try to tighten that middle section.
